html:
[1007]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一声长呼 - A loud, long call or shout, often used to signal or summon.
  • 轰应如雷 - A response that is loud and powerful, like thunder.
  • 乌集鹄立 - A metaphorical expression meaning to gather like crows and stand like herons, describing a scene of people assembling in an orderly manner.
  • 受命皆掩口语，侧耳以听 - Upon receiving a command, they all cover their mouths and listen attentively, indicating a sense of discipline and respect.
  • 客仓卒至 - A guest arrives suddenly or unexpectedly.
  • 十余筵可咄嗟办 - Refers to the ability to prepare a large banquet quickly, emphasizing efficiency and resourcefulness.
  • 肥醴蒸薰 - Describes rich, fragrant wine and food, evoking a sense of luxury and abundance.
  • 纷纷狼藉如雾霈 - Describes a scene of disorder or confusion, like a sudden downpour.
  • 不敢公然蓄歌妓 - Refers to the practice of not openly keeping singing girls, indicating a certain level of discretion or taboo.
  • 狡童十数辈 - Refers to several clever and charming young boys.
  • 慧黠能媚人 - Describes someone who is intelligent, cunning, and able to charm others.
  • 皂纱缠头，唱艳曲 - Refers to young boys wearing black silk headbands and singing romantic songs, indicating a specific cultural practice.
  • 听睹亦颇不恶 - Indicates that the sound and sight are quite pleasant.
  • 金若一出，前后数十骑 - Refers to a person (likely a noble or leader) emerging with dozens of mounted followers.
  • 腰弓矢相摩戛 - Describes the sound of bows and arrows rubbing against each other, indicating a martial or warlike presence.
  • 奴辈呼之皆以“爷” - Refers to servants addressing someone as 'father,' indicating a familial or hierarchical relationship.
  • 即邑人之若民，或“祖”之，“伯、叔”之 - Refers to people in the community addressing someone as 'grandfather,' 'uncle,' or 'aunt,' indicating a complex system of familial or social titles.
  • 不以“师”，不以“上人”，不以禅号也 - Indicates that the person is not addressed as a teacher, a monk, or by a Zen title, suggesting a specific social or religious status.
  • 其徒出，稍稍杀于金 - Refers to the followers of this person being slightly less impressive than the person themselves.
  • 风鬃云辔 - Describes a horse with flowing mane and reins like clouds, indicating a majestic or noble steed.
  • 亦略于贵公子等 - Indicates that the followers are somewhat similar to noble young men.
  • 金又广结纳 - Refers to the person (likely a noble or leader) forming a wide network of connections.
  • 即千里外呼吸亦可通 - Indicates that the person has such a wide network that even someone a thousand miles away can be communicated with.
  • 以此挟方面短长 - Refers to using this network to influence or control regional affairs.
  • 偶气触之，辄惕自惧 - Indicates that if someone offends or provokes the person, they will immediately feel fear or caution.
  • 而其为人，鄙不文 - Describes the person as coarse and lacking in refinement.
  • 顶趾无雅骨 - Indicates that the person lacks noble or refined qualities.
  • 生平不奉一经持一咒 - Refers to the person not following any religious texts or rituals.
  • 迹不履寺院 - Indicates that the person does not visit temples or monasteries.
  • 室中亦未尝蓄铙鼓 - Refers to the person not keeping musical instruments like gongs and drums in their home.
  • 此等物门人辈弗及见，并弗及闻 - Indicates that the disciples or followers of the person have never seen or heard these things.
  • 凡僦屋者，妇女浮丽如京都 - Refers to the women who rent houses being as luxurious and beautiful as those in the capital.
  • 脂泽金粉，皆取给于僧 - Indicates that the women obtain their cosmetics and adornments from monks.
  • 僧亦不之靳，以故里中不田而农者以百数 - Refers to monks not being stingy with these items, resulting in hundreds of people in the village who do not farm but still live comfortably.
  • 时而恶佃决僧首瘗床下 - Indicates that sometimes a wicked tenant would kill a monk and bury his head under the bed.
  • 亦不甚穷诘，但逐去之，其积习然也 - Refers to the fact that this was not heavily investigated, and the person was simply expelled, indicating a long-standing custom or practice.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 细缨革靴者 - Refers to a specific social class or group, likely associated with a certain level of status or profession.
  • 乌集鹄立 - A metaphorical expression that reflects the orderly gathering of people, possibly in a ceremonial or hierarchical context.
  • 肥醴蒸薰 - Describes a luxurious banquet, reflecting the cultural importance of food and drink in social gatherings.
  • 狡童十数辈 - Refers to young boys who are clever and charming, possibly reflecting a cultural practice or social role.
  • 皂纱缠头，唱艳曲 - Describes a specific cultural practice, possibly related to entertainment or social customs.
  • 金若一出，前后数十骑 - Refers to a noble or leader emerging with a retinue, reflecting the hierarchical and martial aspects of society.
  • 奴辈呼之皆以“爷” - Indicates a familial or hierarchical relationship, possibly reflecting a specific social structure.
  • 即邑人之若民，或“祖”之，“伯、叔”之 - Refers to a complex system of familial or social titles, reflecting the importance of lineage and hierarchy.
  • 不以“师”，不以“上人”，不以禅号也 - Indicates a specific social or religious status, possibly reflecting the separation between secular and religious authority.
  • 风鬃云辔 - Describes a majestic or noble steed, reflecting the cultural importance of horses and horsemanship.
  • 金又广结纳 - Refers to the practice of forming wide networks of connections, possibly reflecting the importance of relationships and alliances in society.
  • 以此挟方面短长 - Refers to the use of networks to influence or control regional affairs, reflecting the political and social dynamics of the time.
  • 生平不奉一经持一咒 - Refers to the practice of not following religious texts or rituals, possibly reflecting a specific religious or philosophical stance.
  • 迹不履寺院 - Indicates that the person does not visit temples or monasteries, possibly reflecting a specific religious or philosophical stance.
  • 室中亦未尝蓄铙鼓 - Refers to the person not keeping musical instruments in their home, possibly reflecting a specific cultural or religious practice.
  • 此等物门人辈弗及见，并弗及闻 - Indicates that the disciples or followers of the person have never seen or heard these things, possibly reflecting a specific cultural or religious practice.
  • 凡僦屋者，妇女浮丽如京都 - Refers to the luxurious and beautiful women who rent houses, possibly reflecting the cultural importance of appearance and status.
  • 脂泽金粉，皆取给于僧 - Indicates that the women obtain their cosmetics and adornments from monks, possibly reflecting a specific cultural or religious practice.
  • 僧亦不之靳，以故里中不田而农者以百数 - Refers to monks not being stingy with these items, resulting in hundreds of people in the village who do not farm but still live comfortably, possibly reflecting the economic and social dynamics of the time.
  • 时而恶佃决僧首瘗床下 - Indicates that sometimes a wicked tenant would kill a monk and bury his head under the bed, possibly reflecting the violent or criminal aspects of society.
  • 亦不甚穷诘，但逐去之，其积习然也 - Refers to the fact that this was not heavily investigated, and the person was simply expelled, indicating a long-standing custom or practice, possibly reflecting the legal or social norms of the time.

[1008]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 金又买异姓儿，私子之 - Adoption or fostering of a child from a different surname; common in ancient Chinese families.
  • 延儒师，教帖括业 - Inviting a Confucian teacher to teach the child the classics and imperial examination texts.
  • 儿聪慧能文，因令入邑庠；旋援例作太学生；未几赴北闱，领乡荐 - Sequence of the child's rapid ascent in the educational and official system: entering the local school, becoming a student at the national university, and passing the provincial examination.
  • 由是金之名以“太公”噪 - Jin's reputation spread far and wide, and he was known as 'Tai Gong.'
  • 向之“爷”之者“太”之，膝席者皆垂手执儿孙礼 - Those who once called him 'Father' now called him 'Tai,' and those who used to kneel before him now bowed with the respect due to a grandfather or elder.
  • 无何，太公僧薨 - Not long after, the Grandfather Monk passed away.
  • 孝廉缞绖卧苫块，北面称孤；诸门人释杖满床榻；而灵帏后嘤嘤细泣，惟孝廉夫人一而已 - Mourning rituals following the death of the Grandfather Monk: wearing mourning attire, lying on a mat and a piece of earth, facing north and calling oneself a lonely one, laying down the mourning staffs on the bed.
  • 士大夫妇咸华妆来，搴帏吊唁，冠盖舆马塞道路 - Scholars and their wives all came dressed in fine clothes, lifting the curtains to pay their respects, and the roads were filled with carriages and horses.
  • 殡日，棚阁云连，幡翳日 - On the day of burial, the funeral pavilions stretched like clouds, and the banners and flags blocked the sun.
  • 殉葬刍灵，饰以金帛，舆盖仪仗数十事，马千匹，美人百袂皆如生 - Funeral included straw figures dressed in gold and silk, with dozens of chariots and banners, a thousand horses, and a hundred beautiful women, all made to look lifelike.
  • 方弼、方相，以纸壳制巨人，皂帕金铠，空中而横以木架，纳活人内负之行 - Spirit guides or attendants in the funeral procession, made of paper and cardboard, dressed in black turbans and golden armor, and carried on wooden frames by living people.
  • 设机转动，须眉飞舞，目光铄闪，如将叱咤 - When activated, these figures would move, their beards and eyebrows flying, their eyes shining, as if they were about to shout.
  • 观者惊怪，或小儿女遥望之，辄啼走 - The spectators were amazed and sometimes children would run away when they saw them.
  • 冥宅壮丽如宫阙，楼阁房廊连垣数十亩，千门万户，入者迷不可出 - The underground palace was as magnificent as a palace, with towers and halls connected over dozens of acres, with a thousand doors and ten thousand windows, so intricate that one could get lost inside.
  • 祭品象物，多难指名 - The offerings and symbolic objects were numerous and difficult to name.
  • 会葬者盖相摩，上自方面，皆伛偻入，起拜如朝仪；下至贡监簿史，则手据地以叩，不敢劳公子，劳诸师叔也 - Those attending the funeral were so numerous that they rubbed shoulders, from high officials to low-ranking clerks, all bowing deeply as if in court protocol, while the lower-ranking officials knelt on the ground, showing their respect to the Grandfather Monk.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 孝廉 - Title given to scholars who passed the local examinations and were recommended for higher positions.
  • 太公 - Respectful title for an elderly and respected person, often used for a grandfather or elder statesman.
  • 僧 - Refers to a monk, indicating that Jin held a high rank in the Buddhist clergy.
  • 殡日 - The day of burial.
  • 冥宅 - The afterlife palace, a concept in Chinese funerary practices where the deceased is believed to reside.
  • 方弼、方相 - Spirit guides or attendants in the funeral procession, often depicted as protective figures.
  • 冠盖舆马 - Refers to the carriages and horses of the nobility, symbolizing their status and wealth.
  • 冥宅壮丽如宫阙 - Describes the grandeur of the afterlife palace, reflecting the belief in a luxurious afterlife for the elite.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '私子之' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'adopted him as his own' or 'made him a son.' The context suggests the former.
  • The phrase '帖括业' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'imperial examination texts' or 'classical studies.' The context suggests the former.
  • The phrase '以‘太公’噪' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'became known as 'Tai Gong'' or 'spread the reputation of 'Tai Gong.'' The context suggests the former.
  • The phrase '膝席者皆垂手执儿孙礼' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'those who used to kneel before him now bowed with the respect due to a grandfather or elder' or 'those who used to kneel before him now bowed with the respect due to a grandson or younger relative.' The context suggests the former.
  • The phrase '太公僧' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'Grandfather Monk' or 'Elder Monk.' The context suggests the former.

[1009]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 當是時 - Classical expression meaning 'at that time' or 'at that moment,' often used to introduce a vivid scene or event.
  • 傾國瞻仰 - Hyperbolic phrase meaning 'the whole country looked up in admiration,' emphasizing the widespread attention and spectacle.
  • 男女喘汗屬於道 - Literally 'men and women gasping and sweating along the road,' describing the physical exertion and crowd congestion.
  • 携婦襁儿 - To carry one’s wife and infant child, referring to the act of bringing family members.
  • 呼兄觅妹 - Calling out for brothers and searching for sisters, indicating the confusion and commotion of a crowd.
  • 声鼎沸 - A simile for 'the sound was like a boiling pot,' used to describe a loud, roaring noise.
  • 杂以鼓乐喧豗 - Mixed with the clamor of drums and music, describing the cacophony of sound.
  • 百戏鞺鞳 - A hundred performances resounding with a loud, crashing sound, referring to the noise of various entertainers and performances.
  • 人语都不可闻 - Human speech could not be heard at all, emphasizing the overwhelming noise.
  • 观者自肩以下皆隐不见 - The observers from the shoulders down were all hidden from view, describing how densely packed the crowd was.
  • 惟万顶攒动而已 - Only the movement of countless heads could be seen, reinforcing the image of a crowded, chaotic scene.
  • 有孕妇痛急欲产 - There was a pregnant woman in great pain who was about to give birth, introducing a dramatic and urgent situation.
  • 诸女伴张裙为幄罗守之 - The other women spread out their skirts to make a canopy and guard her, describing a makeshift shelter for the woman in labor.
  • 不暇问雌雄 - No time to ask about the gender, indicating urgency and lack of concern for the child’s sex.
  • 断幅绷怀中 - Tearing a piece of cloth and wrapping it around the infant in the chest, describing the hurried action of swaddling the baby.
  • 或扶之，或曳之，蹩躠以去 - Some supported her, others pulled her, and they limped away, describing the difficult and hurried departure of the group.
  • 奇观哉！ - What a strange sight! A感叹句 (exclamatory sentence) expressing admiration for the scene.
  • 葬后，以金所遗贸产，瓜分而二之 - After his burial, the property left by Jin was divided into two parts, indicating a division of inheritance.
  • 子一，门人一 - One part to the son, one part to the disciples, specifying the division of the estate.
  • 孝廉得半 - The scholar-official (xiāolián) received half, referring to a specific person or title.
  • 而居第之南、之北、之东西，尽缁党 - And the southern, northern, eastern, and western parts of the residence were all occupied by the black-robed faction (缁党), indicating a group of monks or followers.
  • 然皆兄弟叙，痛痒又相关云 - Yet they were all brothers in rank, and their joys and sorrows were also connected, suggesting a familial or close relationship among the groups.
  • 异史氏曰 - A classical phrase used to introduce the author’s commentary or moral reflection, often found in the *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio* (聊斋志异).
  • 此一派也，两宗未有，六祖无传 - This one school, neither of the two sects, and without the transmission of the Sixth Patriarch, referencing Buddhist lineage and sects.
  • 可谓独辟法门者矣 - Can be called one who has uniquely opened a new path (法门), a Buddhist term meaning a method or way to enlightenment.
  • 抑闻之：五蕴皆空，六尘不染，是谓‘和尚’ - But I have heard: When the five aggregates are empty and the six dusts are not tainted, this is called ‘monk’ (和尚), a Buddhist doctrinal statement.
  • 口中说法，座上参禅，是谓‘和样’ - Speaking the Dharma in the mouth, meditating in the seat, this is called ‘he-yang’ (和样), a play on words with the term '和样' (a homophone for '和尚').
  • 鞋香楚地，笠重吴天，是谓‘和撞’ - The scent of shoes in Chu land, the weight of the bamboo hat under Wu’s sky, this is called ‘he-zhuang’ (和撞), another play on words, likely a pun or slang.
  • 鼓钲锽聒，笙管敖曹，是谓‘和唱’ - The clang of drums and gongs, the sound of flutes and pipes, this is called ‘he-chang’ (和唱), a play on the term for 'chanted harmony.'
  • 狗苟钻缘，蝇营淫赌，是谓‘和幛’ - Dog-like scrambling for connections, fly-like chasing of lust and gambling, this is called ‘he-zhang’ (和幛), a term likely used pejoratively or satirically.
  • 金也者，‘尚’耶？‘样’耶？‘唱’耶？‘撞’耶？抑地狱之‘幛’耶？ - Jin, then—‘shang’? ‘yang’? ‘chang’? ‘zhuang’? Or is it the ‘zhang’ of hell? A rhetorical question using the multiple meanings of the characters, likely a satirical or ironic commentary on Jin’s character or legacy.
  • 龙戏蛛 - Dragon plays with a spider, likely the title of the story or a metaphor for the relationship between Jin and others, possibly symbolic or ironic.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 倾国瞻仰 - A phrase that evokes the idea of a national spectacle, similar to 'the whole country looked on' or 'the entire nation watched,' often used in classical Chinese to describe grand events or figures.
  • 孝廉 - A title for a scholar-official who passed the local examination, part of the imperial examination system.
  • 缁党 - Black-robed faction, a term referring to monks or Buddhist followers, as they traditionally wore dark robes.
  • 六祖无传 - Refers to the Sixth Patriarch of Chan (Zen) Buddhism, Huineng, and the idea that his teachings were not passed down in this tradition, suggesting a break in the lineage.
  • 五蕴皆空 - A core Buddhist concept meaning 'the five aggregates are empty,' referring to the idea that all material and mental components of existence are impermanent and not self.
  • 六尘不染 - Six dusts are not tainted, referring to the six sense objects (sight, sound, smell, taste, touch, and mind) that are not tainted by attachment.
  • 和尚 - Monk, a term that is also used in the text in a play on words with the other terms.
  • 和样 - A play on words with the term '和样' (a homophone for '和尚').
  • 和撞 - Another play on words, likely a pun or slang.
  • 和唱 - A play on the term for 'chanted harmony.'
  • 和幛 - A term likely used pejoratively or satirically.
  • 地狱之‘幛’ - The ‘zhang’ of hell, a further satirical or ironic twist, suggesting that Jin’s actions or legacy are as bad as hell itself.
  • 龙戏蛛 - The title of the story, possibly metaphorical, suggesting a powerful figure (the dragon) playing with a weak or insignificant one (the spider), or a symbolic relationship between Jin and others.

[1010]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 用藏肴饵 - used to store food and bait
  • 往往被物窃食 - often stolen by animals
  • 家人屡受谯责 - the family members often received severe reprimands
  • 因伏伺之 - therefore they lay in wait to observe it
  • 公以为异 - the magistrate considered it unusual
  • 日遣婢辈投饵焉 - he sent maidservants daily to offer food
  • 蛛益驯 - the spider became even more tame
  • 饥辄出依人 - when hungry, it would come out to rely on humans
  • 饱而后去 - after eating, it would leave
  • 积年余 - for several years
  • 公偶阅案牍 - the magistrate happened to read through the official documents
  • 蛛忽来伏几上 - the spider suddenly came and lay on the desk
  • 疑其饥 - he suspected it was hungry
  • 方呼家人取饵 - he just called the family to fetch food
  • 旋见两蛇夹蛛卧 - soon he saw two snakes lying beside the spider
  • 细裁如箸 - thin like chopsticks
  • 蛛爪蜷腹缩 - the spider curled its claws and contracted its body
  • 若不胜惧 - as if unable to bear fear
  • 转瞬间 - in an instant
  • 蛇暴长粗于卵 - the snakes suddenly grew as large as eggs
  • 大骇欲走 - greatly frightened and wanted to run away
  • 巨霆大作 - a huge thunderstorm erupted
  • 合家震毙 - the whole family was killed by the shock
  • 移时公苏 - after some time, the magistrate revived
  • 夫人及婢仆击死者七人 - his wife and maidservants were struck dead by seven people
  • 公病月余 - the magistrate was ill for over a month
  • 寻卒 - soon died

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 徐公为齐东令 - Magistrate Xu of Qidong
  • 署中 - in the office
  • 齐东 - Qidong, a place name in ancient China
  • 龙戏蛛 - dragon playing with the spider, a metaphorical reference to a mythological or supernatural event
  • 雷霆之击 - thunder strike, a common natural phenomenon often associated with divine punishment in Chinese culture
  • 异史氏曰 - The historian said, a term often used in classical Chinese to denote a historian or commentator

Proper Nouns

  • 徐公 - the name of the magistrate
  • 齐东 - a place name in ancient China
  • 异史氏 - a term often used in classical Chinese to denote a historian or commentator

[1011]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 天津商人某 - Refers to a merchant from Tianjin, where '天津' means 'Tianjin' and '商人' means 'merchant.'
  • 将贾远方 - Means 'intending to trade in distant places,' with '贾' meaning 'trade' or 'do business.'
  • 往从富人贷资数百 - Means 'went to borrow hundreds of units of currency from a wealthy person,' with '贷资' meaning 'borrow money.'
  • 为偷儿所窥 - Means 'was seen by a thief,' with '为...所' indicating passivity and '偷儿' meaning 'thief.'
  • 及夕，预匿室中以俟其归 - Means 'by evening, he had already hidden himself in the room to wait for his return,' with '及夕' meaning 'by evening' and '俟' meaning 'await.'
  • 但闻商人妇转侧床上，似不成眠 - Means 'he only heard the merchant's wife turning and tossing in bed, as if unable to sleep,' with '转侧' meaning 'turning and tossing' and '不成眠' meaning 'unable to sleep.'
  • 壁上一小门开，一室尽亮 - Means 'a small door in the wall opened, and the entire room became bright,' with '尽亮' meaning 'became completely bright.'
  • 手引长带一条，近榻授妇 - Means 'she held out a long strip of cloth, approaching the bed to give it to the wife,' with '引' meaning 'hold out' and '授' meaning 'give.'
  • 妇以手却之 - Means 'the wife pushed it away with her hand,' with '却' meaning 'push away.'
  • 女固授之 - Means 'the girl insisted on giving it,' with '固' meaning 'insistently.'
  • 妇乃受带，起悬梁上，引颈自缢 - Means 'the wife then took the strip, stood up, hung it on the beam, and strangled herself,' with '悬' meaning 'hang' and '自缢' meaning 'strangle oneself.'
  • 偷儿大惊，拔关遁去 - Means 'the thief was greatly shocked, pulled open the door, and fled,' with '拔关' meaning 'pull open the door' and '遁去' meaning 'fled.'
  • 既明，家人见妇死 - Means 'when it was light, the family saw the wife dead,' with '既明' meaning 'when it was light.'
  • 质诸官 - Means 'they reported it to the authorities,' with '质' meaning 'report' and '诸官' meaning 'authorities.'
  • 官拘邻人而锻炼之 - Means 'the officials arrested the neighbors and interrogated them,' with '拘' meaning 'arrest' and '锻炼' meaning 'interrogate.'
  • 诬服成狱，不日就决 - Means 'they were falsely convicted, and the case was decided quickly,' with '诬服' meaning 'falsely convicted' and '就决' meaning 'decided.'
  • 偷儿愤其冤，自首于堂 - Means 'the thief, angry at the injustice, confessed in the courtroom,' with '愤其冤' meaning 'angry at the injustice' and '自首' meaning 'confess.'
  • 鞫之情真 - Means 'the truth was investigated,' with '鞫' meaning 'investigate' and '情真' meaning 'truth.'
  • 邻人遂免 - Means 'the neighbors were then acquitted,' with '遂免' meaning 'were then acquitted.'
  • 问其里人 - Means 'they asked the people of the neighborhood,' with '里人' meaning 'people of the neighborhood.'
  • 言宅之故主曾有少妇经死 - Means 'they said that the former owner of the house once had a young woman who died,' with '故主' meaning 'former owner' and '经死' meaning 'died.'
  • 年齿容貌，与盗言悉符 - Means 'her age, appearance, and all the details matched what the thief had said,' with '年齿' meaning 'age,' '容貌' meaning 'appearance,' and '悉符' meaning 'all match.'
  • 因知是其鬼也 - Means 'thus they knew it was her ghost,' with '因知' meaning 'thus knew' and '鬼' meaning 'ghost.'
  • 欲传暴死者必求代替 - Means 'it seems that those who die violently must seek someone to take their place,' with '传暴死者' meaning 'those who die violently' and '必求代替' meaning 'must seek someone to take their place.'
  • 其然欤 - Means 'is this true?' with '其然' meaning 'is it true' and '欤' being a question particle.
  • 阎罗宴 - Means 'Yanluo Banquet,' with '阎罗' referring to Yanluo, the king of hell in Chinese mythology, and '宴' meaning 'banquet.'

  • 李击节不置 - ‘李’ is Li, ‘击节’ means ‘tap the rhythm’, and ‘不置’ means ‘not stop’, so the phrase means ‘Li tapped the rhythm without stopping’.

  • 若得荆人一奏 - ‘若’ means ‘if’, ‘得’ means ‘get’, ‘荆人’ means ‘a woman from Jing’, and ‘一奏’ means ‘one performance’, so the phrase means ‘if one gets a performance from a woman from Jing’.
  • 当有一两声可听者 - ‘当’ means ‘there will be’, ‘一两声’ means ‘one or two sounds’, ‘可听者’ means ‘that can be heard’, so the phrase means ‘there will be one or two sounds that can be heard’.

The sarcastic remark from the magistrate is '想其赂未到耳,' and the outcome is shown by '遂责吏，释邑人.' The warning from the townsman is '要知狼诈多端，此辈败我阴，甚至丧我身家,' and the sarcastic comment from the townsman is '不知居官者作何心腑，偏要以赤子饲麻胡也.' Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 忽欲歸寧，要黃御送之 - This sentence introduces a sudden desire and the invitation for escort, which must be read together.
  • 向言無家，何前後之舛 - The question about inconsistency requires understanding the context of previous statements.
  • 曩漫言之 - The casual nature of the previous statement adds nuance to the narrative.
  • 妾家頗裕，君竭資而往，必無相虧 - This conditional statement must be read as a promise of no financial loss.
  • 要商子過，驚其絕，反舟綴之，而黃不知也 - The sequence of events involving the merchant's son must be read as a continuous action.
  • 妾相從數年，未能為君育男女，亦一不了事 - The statement about the inability to have children adds emotional depth to the narrative.
  • 妾雖陋，幸未老耄，有能以千金相贈者，便鬻妾去，此中妻室、田廬皆備焉 - The offer to sell herself must be read as a desperate measure.
  • 此計如何 - The question about the plan must be read as a moment of hesitation and uncertainty.
  • 失色 - The reaction of the male character must be read as a moment of shock or disbelief.
  • 天下固多佳人，誰肯以千金買妾者 - The rhetorical question about the rarity of such offers must be read as a reflection on the situation.
  • 戲言於外，以覘其有無 - The plan to test the waters must be read as a strategic move.
  • 賣不賣，固自在君耳 - The final decision must be read as a matter of personal choice.

  • 心爱敬之 - He loved and respected him, showing a change in Wang's attitude.
  • 展问邦族，云：“登州宋姓。” - He asked about his family and clan, and he said, 'I am from Dengzhou, the surname is Song,' introducing the young man's background.
  • 因命苍头设座，相对噱谈 - He then ordered the servant to set up a seat, and they sat opposite each other, joking and chatting, indicating a friendly interaction.
  • 余杭生适过 - The native of Yuhang happened to pass by, introducing another character.
  • 共起逊坐 - They both stood up and made way for him to sit, showing a gesture of respect.
  • 生居然上座，更不撝挹 - The native sat in the upper seat without being humble, indicating a lack of humility.
  • 卒然问宋：“亦入闱者耶？” - Suddenly, he asked Song, 'Are you also taking the examination?' showing a direct question.
  • 答曰：“非也。驽骀之才，无志腾骧久矣。” - Song replied, 'No. I have the talent of a weak horse, and I have long lost the ambition to rise.' indicating a self-deprecating response.
  • 又问：“何省？” - He then asked, 'Which province?' showing further questioning.
  • 宋告之 - Song told him, indicating a response.
  • 生曰：“竟不进取，足知高明。山左、右并无一字通者。” - The native said, 'Not seeking advancement, it is enough to know your intelligence. There are no scholars in the left and right mountains.' indicating a judgment on Song's intelligence and the local scholarly environment.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 数日榜放 - Refers to the traditional practice of announcing the results of the imperial examinations, which were crucial for selecting officials in ancient China.
  • 生竟领荐 - Indicates success in the examination, which was a significant achievement for scholars.

  • 走告僧 - The act of informing the monk, who is likely a respected figure with knowledge of the examination system.
  • 盲于目 - A reference to the monk's physical blindness, but also a metaphor for his keen insight into the quality of writing.
  • 帘中人并鼻盲矣 - A critique of the examiners, suggesting their incompetence in evaluating the candidates' work.
  • 啖人水角 - A metaphorical expression that reflects the competitive and often cutthroat nature of the examination system.
  • 剜我盲瞳去 - A dramatic and hyperbolic threat, emphasizing the monk's strong reaction to the situation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 稱得金八百余两 - Literal expression meaning 'claimed to have found eight hundred and more taels of gold'; conveys surprise and joy.
  • 固辞乃已 - Formal expression meaning 'refused firmly and only then did he stop'; indicates strong refusal followed by cessation.
  • 积数月，敦习益苦 - Compound expression meaning 'after several months, the diligent practice became increasingly arduous'; conveys sustained effort leading to greater difficulty.
  • 欲与瓜分 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'wanted to divide it equally'; '瓜分' (dividing like cutting a melon) is a common idiom for fair division.
  • 始真是命矣 - Colloquial expression meaning 'it's truly fate'; suggests that the outcome is inevitable and determined by destiny.
  • 恐相惊怪 - Polite expression meaning 'fear that you might be startled or surprised'; used to soften the impact of a potentially shocking revelation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 久之 - Classical phrase indicating the passage of time; translated as 'After a long time' or 'Eventually.'

  • 又惧十日复来 - Yet feared she would return in ten days, indicating continued fear.
  • 乃货婢鬻衣 - So they sold their servant and clothes; '货婢' means to sell a maid, and '鬻衣' to sell clothes.
  • 以足其数 - To make up the amount, indicating the purpose of the sale.
  • 至期女果至 - When the time came, the woman indeed arrived, showing fulfillment of expectation.

  • 自此遂绝 - From then on, it ceased, marking the end of the interaction.
  • 生足创，医药半年始愈 - The man's foot wound required six months of medical treatment to heal, indicating prolonged recovery.

  • 三年间援例纳粟 - Within three years, they followed the example and paid grain taxes; '援例' means to follow precedent.
  • 夏屋连蔓 - Their summer houses were connected in a row, indicating prosperity.
  • 所衣华服半生家物 - The clothes they wore were half from their own family’s wealth, indicating their new status.
  • 主见之，亦不敢问 - The master saw this but dared not ask, suggesting fear or deference.
  • 偶适野，遇女于途 - Once, while traveling in the wild, he met the woman on the road; '适野' means to go into the countryside.
  • 长跪道左 - He knelt long on the left side of the road, a gesture of deep respect or submission.
  • 女无言，但以素巾裹五六金，遥掷之 - The woman said nothing, but wrapped five or six taels of gold in a plain silk cloth and threw it from afar, indicating a gesture of generosity.
  • 反身径去 - Turned around and left straightaway, showing abrupt departure.
  • 后于氏早卒 - Later, the Yu family died early, indicating premature death.
  • 女犹时至其家 - The woman still occasionally visited their home, showing continued presence.
  • 家中金帛辄亡去 - Whenever they were in the house, the gold and silk would disappear, indicating continued theft.
  • 于子睹其来，拜参之 - The son of Yu saw her coming and bowed to her respectfully, showing submission.
  • 女去，遂不复至 - The woman left, and never returned again, marking the end of the relationship.
  • 异史氏曰 - A standard phrase used in *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio* to introduce the author’s commentary or moral reflection.
  • 邪物之来，杀之亦壮 - The coming of a demon or evil spirit—killing it is also brave, indicating a moral dilemma.
  • 而既受其德，即鬼物不可负也 - But once one has received their kindness, even a ghost or spirit cannot be betrayed, emphasizing moral obligation.
  • 既贵而杀赵孟，则贤豪非之矣 - If one becomes rich and kills Zhao Meng, the virtuous and noble will criticize you, alluding to a historical or literary reference.
  • 夫人非其心之所好，即万锺何动焉 - If something is not what one’s heart desires, even ten thousand measures of grain will not move one, indicating the importance of inner motivation.
  • 观其见金色喜，其亦利之所在，丧身辱行而不惜者欤？ - Observing that she was pleased by the sight of gold, is she not one who, for the sake of profit, would not hesitate to lose her body and honor? A moral critique.
  • 伤哉贪人，卒取残败！ - Alas, the greedy person—eventually, they are left in ruin! A conclusion on the futility of greed.
  • 吕无病 - Likely the name of the author or a contributor, though not commonly known in classical Chinese literature; may be a pseudonym or a lesser-known figure.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 狐 - 'Fox' in Chinese literature often symbolizes a *huli jing* (狐仙), a fox spirit or shapeshifter with supernatural powers.
  • 于氏 - A surname, likely referring to a family in the story.
  • 纳粟 - Paying grain taxes, a common practice in feudal China.
  • 夏屋连蔓 - 'Summer houses connected in a row' refers to the expansion of a family's estate or wealth.
  • 华服 - 'Fine clothing' or 'luxurious attire,' indicating wealth and social mobility.
  • 异史氏 - A standard title used by the author of *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio* (聊斋志异) to introduce his own commentary or moral reflection.
  • 赵孟 - Likely a reference to *Zhao Meng* (赵孟), possibly a historical or literary figure, though not explicitly identified here. The phrase '杀赵孟' may allude to a moral or historical judgment.
  • 万锺 - A unit of grain, used metaphorically to mean great wealth.
  • 吕无病 - Likely the name of the compiler or author of the story, though not widely known in classical Chinese literature. Could be a pseudonym or a lesser-known contributor.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 留則留之，否則死之 - Conditional statement meaning 'If you keep me, then I will stay; otherwise, I will die.' Reflects a dramatic ultimatum.
  • 誠有十分惡，寧無一分情 - Rhetorical question meaning 'If there is indeed ten parts of evil, how could there be no one part of affection?' Emphasizes the speaker's emotional attachment despite perceived flaws.
  • 香火之誓 - Refers to a solemn vow or promise, often made in the context of religious or familial bonds.
  • 袖覆其上 - Means 'to cover it with her sleeve,' indicating a gesture of protection or concealment.
  • 特借斗室為出家計 - Means 'just borrowing this small room for my monastic life,' suggesting a temporary or transitional state.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 二十一歲從君，二十三歲被出 - Refers to a woman’s marriage and subsequent divorce, highlighting the social and personal consequences of such events in ancient China.
  • 妾 - Refers to a concubine or lower-ranking wife, emphasizing her subordinate status.
  • 黃梁之夢 - A metaphor for a fleeting or illusory dream, often used to describe a brief period of happiness or illusion.
  • 斗室 - Refers to a small room, often used metaphorically to describe a humble or confined space.
  • 出家 - Refers to entering a religious life, often associated with Buddhism or Daoism.

  • 家政废弛 - Literal meaning 'the household administration is neglected,' indicating a decline in the management of the household.
  • 腆颜一经纪之 - Literal meaning 'with a humble face, take charge of it,' suggesting a reluctant but necessary return to managing the household affairs.

[1118]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 孫大怒，挞儿 - Grandfather became very angry and beat the boy; '大怒' conveys strong anger.
  • 妇苏，力止之，且喜曰 - The wife woke up, tried her best to stop him, and happily said; '且喜' indicates a mix of relief and joy.
  • 妾昔虐儿，中心每不自释 - I used to mistreat the boy and always felt guilty in my heart; '中心每不自释' conveys a deep sense of guilt.
  • 今幸销一罪案矣 - Now I am fortunate to have erased one sin; '销一罪案' is a metaphor for atoning for a wrongdoing.
  • 孙益嬖爱之，妇每拒，使就妾宿 - Grandfather became even more fond of her, the wife always refused, and made her stay with the concubine; '益嬖爱' conveys an increase in affection.
  • 居数年，屡产屡殇 - After several years, she gave birth repeatedly but the babies died; '屡产屡殇' conveys a tragic pattern of repeated loss.
  • 此昔日杀儿之报也 - This is the retribution for killing the boy in the past; '昔日杀儿之报' conveys a sense of karmic retribution.
  • 阿坚既娶，遂以外事委儿，内事委媳 - Ah Jian was married, and entrusted external affairs to the boy and internal affairs to the wife; '委儿' and '委媳' convey delegation of responsibilities.
  • 一日曰：“妾某日当死。”孙不信 - One day she said, 'I will die on a certain day,' and Grandfather did not believe her; '某日当死' conveys a specific prediction of death.
  • 妇自理葬具，至日更衣入棺而卒 - The wife prepared her own funeral items, on that day, she changed clothes, entered the coffin, and died; '更衣入棺而卒' conveys a ritualistic and symbolic act of death.
  • 颜色如生，异香满室；既殓，香始渐灭 - Her appearance was as if she were still alive, a strange fragrance filled the room; after the coffin was closed, the fragrance gradually faded; '异香满室' conveys a supernatural or spiritual element.
  • 异史氏曰 - The Strange Storyteller says or The historian remarks; '异史氏曰' is a common phrase in classical Chinese literature.
  • 心之所好，原不在妍媸也 - What the heart desires is not necessarily in beauty or ugliness; '原不在妍媸也' conveys a philosophical reflection on the nature of desire.
  • 毛嫱、西施，焉知非自爱之者美之乎？ - Who knows if those who love themselves do not see them as beautiful?; '焉知非自爱之者美之乎' conveys a philosophical reflection on beauty and self-love.
  • 然不遭悍妒，其贤不彰 - However, without encountering fierce jealousy, her virtues would not be revealed; '其贤不彰' conveys a sense of hidden virtue.
  • 几令人与嗜痂者并笑矣 - Almost makes one laugh along with those who have a taste for scabs; '嗜痂者' is a metaphor for someone with an unusual or perverse taste.
  • 至锦屏之人，其夙根原厚，故豁然一悟，立证菩提 - As for the woman behind the screen, her innate virtue was originally deep, so she suddenly understood and immediately attained enlightenment; '立证菩提' conveys a spiritual awakening.
  • 若地狱道中，皆富贵而不经艰难者矣 - If in the realm of hell, all are rich and noble but have not endured hardships; '皆富贵而不经艰难者矣' conveys a critique of those who have not faced adversity.
  • 钱卜巫 - Qian divined with a scaphe; '钱卜' refers to a divination method using a scaphe (a type of shell).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 孙大怒，挞儿 - Reflects a traditional family structure and the authority of elders.

  • 心之所好，原不在妍媸也 - Reflects the influence of Confucian and Daoist thought on the nature of desire.
  • 毛嫱、西施，焉知非自爱之者美之乎？ - Reflects the cultural significance of beauty and self-love.
  • 然不遭悍妒，其贤不彰 - Reflects the moral and ethical values of the time regarding jealousy.
  • 几令人与嗜痂者并笑矣 - Reflects the cultural significance of unusual or perverse tastes.
  • 至锦屏之人，其夙根原厚，故豁然一悟，立证菩提 - Reflects the influence of Buddhist thought on spiritual awakening.
  • 若地狱道中，皆富贵而不经艰难者矣 - Reflects a critique of those who have not faced adversity.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 商恪遵治命 - 商 (Shang) refers to a person, and 恪遵治命 (ke zun zhi ming) means 'strictly obeying the will of the deceased.' This phrase indicates that 商 is adhering to the wishes of someone who has passed away.
  • 誠朴無二 - This phrase means 'sincere and simple without deceit,' describing 商's character.
  • 躬耕自给 - 躬耕 (gong geng) means 'tilling the land personally,' and 自给 (zi ji) means 'self-sufficient.' Together, this phrase describes 商's lifestyle of working the land to sustain himself.
  • 乡人咸爱敬之 - 乡人 (xiang ren) means 'villagers,' 咸 (xian) means 'all,' 爱敬 (ai jing) means 'love and respect,' and 之 (zhi) is a pronoun referring to 商. This phrase indicates that all the villagers love and respect 商.
  • 假以资使学负贩 - 假 (jia) means 'to lend,' 资 (zi) means 'money or capital,' 使 (shi) means 'to make,' 学 (xue) means 'to learn,' and 负贩 (fu fan) means 'to carry goods for sale.' This phrase means that a wealthy man lent 商 money to learn how to carry goods for sale.
  • 辄亏其母 - 辄 (zhe) means 'always,' 亏 (kui) means 'to harm or damage,' and 其母 (qi mu) means 'his mother.' This phrase indicates that 商 always harmed his mother's interests.
  • 愧无以偿 - 愧 (kui) means 'ashamed,' 无以 (wu yi) means 'unable to,' and 偿 (chang) means 'to repay.' This phrase means that 商 felt ashamed because he could not repay the debt.
  • 请为佣 - 请 (qing) means 'to request,' 为 (wei) means 'to be,' and 佣 (yong) means 'a hired worker.' This phrase means that 商 requested to become a hired worker.
  • 翁不肯 - 翁 (weng) means 'old man' or 'elderly man,' and 不肯 (bu ken) means 'refused.' This phrase indicates that the elderly man refused 商's request.
  • 商瞿然不自安 - 商 (Shang) is the name of the person, 瞿然 (qu ran) means 'suddenly or startled,' and 不自安 (bu zi an) means 'feeling uneasy.' This phrase describes 商's reaction to the situation.
  • 尽货其田宅 - 尽 (jin) means 'to sell everything,' 货 (huo) means 'to sell,' 其 (qi) is a pronoun referring to 商's, 田宅 (tian zhai) means 'land and houses.' This phrase means that 商 sold all his land and houses.
  • 往酬翁 - 往 (wang) means 'to go,' 酬 (chou) means 'to repay,' and 翁 (weng) means 'elderly man.' This phrase means that 商 went to repay the elderly man.
  • 翁请得情 - 翁 (weng) means 'elderly man,' 请 (qing) means 'to ask,' 得 (de) means 'to understand,' and 情 (qing) means 'the situation.' This phrase means that the elderly man understood the situation.
  • 益直之 - 益 (yi) means 'more,' 直 (zhi) means 'to increase,' and 之 (zhi) is a pronoun referring to the repayment. This phrase means that the elderly man increased the repayment.
  • 强为赎还旧业 - 强 (qiang) means 'to force,' 为 (wei) means 'to do,' 赎还 (shu huan) means 'to redeem,' and 旧业 (jiu ye) means 'old business or property.' This phrase means that the elderly man forced 商 to redeem his old property.
  • 又益贷以重金 - 又 (you) means 'again,' 益 (yi) means 'more,' 贷 (dai) means 'to lend,' 以 (yi) means 'with,' and 重金 (zhong jin) means 'a large sum of money.' This phrase means that the elderly man lent 商 a large sum of money again.
  • 俾作贾 - 俾 (bi) means 'to make,' 作 (zuo) means 'to do,' and 贾 (gua) means 'merchant.' This phrase means that the elderly man made 商 become a merchant.
  • 十数金尚不能偿 - 十数金 (shi shu jin) means 'a dozen or so gold coins,' 尚 (shang) means 'still,' 不能 (bu neng) means 'cannot,' and 偿 (chang) means 'to repay.' This phrase means that 商 still could not repay a dozen or so gold coins.
  • 奈何结来生驴马债耶 - 奈何 (nai he) means 'how can,' 结 (jie) means 'to tie,' 来生 (lai sheng) means 'next life,' 驴马债 (lu ma zhai) means 'debt of donkeys and horses,' and 耶 (ye) is a particle used at the end of a question. This phrase means that 商 asked how he could tie up a debt of donkeys and horses in his next life.
  • 再强之 - 再 (zai) means 'again,' 强 (qiang) means 'to force,' and 之 (zhi) is a pronoun referring to 商. This phrase means that the elderly man tried to force 商 again.
  • 必不可 - 必 (bi) means 'certainly,' 不 (bu) means 'not,' and 可 (ke) means 'can.' This phrase means that 商 certainly could not do it.
  • 躬耕如故 - 躬耕 (gong geng) means 'tilling the land personally,' and 如故 (ru gu) means 'as before.' This phrase means that 商 tilled the land as before.
  • 每自叹曰 - 每 (mei) means 'every,' 自 (zi) means 'himself,' 叹 (tan) means 'to sigh,' and 曰 (yue) means 'said.' This phrase means that 商 often sighed and said.
  • 人生世上，皆有数年之享 - 人生世上 (ren sheng shi shang) means 'life on earth,' 皆 (jie) means 'all,' 有 (you) means 'have,' 数年 (shu nian) means 'a few years,' and 享 (xiang) means 'enjoyment.' This phrase means that all people have a few years of enjoyment.
  • 何遂落魄如此 - 何遂 (he sui) means 'why did,' 落魄 (luo po) means 'to be destitute,' and 如此 (ru ci) means 'like this.' This phrase means that 商 wondered why he was so destitute.
  • 会有外来巫 - 会 (hui) means 'there happened to be,' 有 (you) means 'have,' 外来 (wai lai) means 'from outside,' and 巫 (wu) means 'a fortune-teller.' This phrase means that there happened to be a fortune-teller from outside.
  • 以钱卜 - 以 (yi) means 'with,' 钱 (qian) means 'money,' and 卜 (bu) means 'to divinate.' This phrase means that the fortune-teller used money to divinate.
  • 悉知人运数 - 悉 (xi) means 'all,' 知 (zhi) means 'to know,' 人 (ren) means 'people,' and 运数 (yun shu) means 'destiny.' This phrase means that the fortune-teller knew all about people's destinies.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 商 - A person's name, likely a commoner.
  • 翁 - Refers to an elderly man, often used to show respect.
  • 田宅 - Land and houses, important assets in traditional Chinese society.
  • 负贩 - Carrying goods for sale, a common occupation for the lower class.
  • 佣 - A hired worker, often used to describe someone who works for wages.
  • 贾 - Merchant, a person who buys and sells goods.
  • 驴马债 - A metaphorical expression for a heavy debt, often used to describe a significant financial burden.
  • 巫 - Fortune-teller, a person who practices divination and other spiritual practices.
  • 钱卜 - Divination using money, a practice that was common in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 运数 - Destiny, a concept that suggests that one's fate is predetermined.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 敬诣之 - Respectful way of saying 'to visit' or 'to go to'; indicates the protagonist's respectful approach.

  • 中设神座 - Refers to the central placement of a shrine or altar; suggests a sacred space.

  • 商人朝拜讫 - Means 'the merchant has finished his morning worship'; shows a ritualistic aspect.
  • 巫便索资 - The witch then asks for payment; indicates a transactional element.
  • 商授百钱 - The merchant gives the witch a hundred coins; specifies the amount.
  • 巫尽纳木筒中 - The witch places all the money in a wooden tube; suggests a ritual or symbolic act.
  • 执跪座下 - The witch kneels in front of the shrine; indicates a submissive or reverent posture.
  • 摇响如祈签状 - The witch shakes something to produce a sound, similar to drawing a lot; suggests a divination ritual.
  • 已而起 - Afterward, she stands up; marks the end of the ritual.
  • 倾钱入手 - She pours the money into her hand; indicates a transfer of ownership.
  • 而后于案上次第摆之 - Then she arranges the coins on the table in a specific order; suggests a methodical or ritualistic process.
  • 其法以字为否，幕为亨 - The method is to interpret the arrangement of the coins, where '字' (heads) means '否' (no) and '幕' (tails) means '亨' (yes).
  • 数至五十八皆字，以后则尽幕矣 - Up to the 58th coin, all are heads, after which they are all tails; indicates a pattern or prophecy.
  • 遂问 - The merchant then asks a question; initiates a dialogue.
  • 庚甲几何 - This phrase is a bit ambiguous; could mean 'What is the year?' or 'How many years?' depending on the context.
  • 早矣 - Means 'too early' or 'too soon'; indicates a sense of urgency or disappointment.
  • 官人现行者先人运，非身运 - The merchant's current luck is that of his ancestors, not his own; suggests a generational influence.
  • 五十八岁方交本身运 - At the age of 58, he will experience his own luck; indicates a turning point.
  • 始无盘错也 - He will then be free from difficulties; suggests a resolution of problems.
  • 何谓先人运 - The merchant asks what '先人运' means; seeks clarification.
  • 先人有善，其福未尽，则后人享之；先人有不善，其祸未尽，则后人亦受之 - Explains that the good or bad deeds of ancestors have lingering effects on their descendants; indicates a belief in generational karma.
  • 屈指曰 - The merchant counts on his fingers; indicates a calculation or reflection.
  • 再三十年，齿已老耆，行就木矣 - In another thirty years, he will be old and near death; suggests a sense of mortality.
  • 五十八以前，便有回国，略可营谋 - Before the age of 58, he will return home and can make some plans; indicates a period of preparation.
  • 然仅免饥寒耳 - However, he will only avoid hunger and cold; suggests limited success.
  • 五十八之年，当有巨金自来，不须力求 - At the age of 58, a large sum of money will come to him without effort; indicates a stroke of luck.
  • 官人生无过行，再世享之不尽也 - The merchant's good deeds will bring him endless benefits in future lives; suggests a belief in reincarnation and karma.
  • 疑信半焉 - The merchant is half doubtful and half believing; indicates a state of uncertainty.
  • 安贫自守，不敢妄求 - He remains poor but content, not daring to seek more; suggests a sense of resignation or contentment.
  • 后至五十三岁，留意验之 - After reaching 53, he pays attention to verifying the prophecy; indicates a turning point in his attitude.
  • 时方东作，病痁不能耕 - At the time, he was engaged in spring farming but fell ill with malaria and could not work; suggests a period of hardship.
  • 既痊，天大旱，早禾尽枯 - After recovering, there was a severe drought, and the early rice crops all withered; indicates a natural disaster.
  • 近秋方雨，家无别种，田数亩悉以种谷 - Just before autumn, it rained, but his family had no other seeds, so he planted the few acres of land with rice; suggests a desperate attempt to salvage the harvest.

  • 尝语人曰：“吾有此乐，翰苑不易也。” - He once said to others, 'I have this kind of happiness, which is not easy to find in the imperial library,' indicating his contentment with his current life.
  • 异史氏曰 - The historian said, introducing a commentary.
  • 余每至张夫子庙堂，瞻其须眉，凛禀有生气 - I often visit the temple of Master Zhang, and when I look at his mustache and eyebrows, they seem to have a living presence, indicating a sense of reverence.
  • 又其生平喑哑如霹雳声，矛马所至，无不大快，出人意表 - His life was as silent as thunder, and wherever he went with his spear and horse, everything was made clear and understandable, beyond what people expected, describing his character and actions.
  • 世以将军好武，遂置与绛，灌伍，宁知文昌事繁，须侯固多哉 - The world considers generals to be good at war, so they are placed among the ranks of Jiang and Guan (historical figures), but who knows that the affairs of the imperial library are complex and require many capable individuals, indicating a contrast between military and scholarly roles.
  • 呜呼！三十五年，来何暮也 - Alas! Thirty-five years have passed, and it feels like it came too late, expressing a sense of regret and reflection.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 孙必振 - The name of the protagonist, a historical figure or a person of note.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '值大风雷，舟船荡摇' describes the storm and the boat's condition, which can be read as two separate events or as a single, continuous event.
  • The action of '手持金字牌下示' can be interpreted as a single, continuous action or as two distinct actions.
  • The clause '众谓孙：“必汝有犯天谴，请自为一舟，勿相累。”' can be read as a direct quote from the crowd to Sun, emphasizing the urgency and seriousness of the situation.
  • The sequence of actions '孙尚无言，众不待其肯可，视旁有小舟，共推置其上' can be read as a series of quick, decisive actions taken by the crowd without waiting for Sun's response.
  • The description '后有邻翁来市肉，苦争低昂，添脂搭肉，片片碎割，其苦更惨' describes the neighbor's experience in buying meat, which can be read as a series of actions or as a continuous, painful process.

Proper Nouns
